Sinhagad- Gad Ala, Pan Sinh Gela

[Maratha History](https://sadashree.substack.com/s/maratha-history/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

# Sinhagad- Gad Ala, Pan Sinh Gela

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Feb 03, 2026

Share

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!mVGU!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F109b5ecf-9e64-4e33-8e37-ad639484b33d_250x302.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!mVGU!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F109b5ecf-9e64-4e33-8e37-ad639484b33d_250x302.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The capture of Sinhagad in 1670 is one of those episodes that feels less like history and more like legend. Tanaji Malusare, Shivaji’s trusted commander, led the daring night assault on the fort, scaling its steep cliffs with ropes and grappling hooks. The battle was fierce, and though Tanaji fell in combat, his sacrifice ensured victory. Shivaji’s words afterwar, *“Gad ala, pan Sinha gela”* (“The fort is won, but the lion is lost”, still echo as a testament to both triumph and grief.

What makes this operation remarkable is not just the military brilliance but the spirit behind it. The Marathas fought with a sense of mission that went beyond conquest, it was about reclaiming dignity, resisting imperial domination, and building a homeland where Hindus and Muslims alike could find respect under Shivaji’s rule.

Walking through Pune and its surrounding forts,Raigad, Purandar, Pratapgarh, you’re not just tracing stones and ruins, you’re tracing the pulse of a civilizational moment. Each fort is a chapter in a larger saga, Raigad the seat of coronation, Pratapgarh the site of Afzal Khan’s defeat, Purandar the stage of treaty and resilience. Together, they form a constellation of courage that gave Aurangzeb sleepless nights and forced him to reckon with a leader who fought not only with the sword but with vision.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ptKM!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Faad964ec-556a-4619-8499-b84e4e7ae271_517x358.jpeg)

![sin](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ptKM!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Faad964ec-556a-4619-8499-b84e4e7ae271_517x358.jpeg "sin")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Sinhagad really does stand apart in its sheer drama. Originally Kondhana, it sits like a sentinel in the Bhuleswar range of the Sahyadris, about 25 km from Pune, perched at nearly 4,400 feet above sea level. To the east and west, it links with Purandar through high ridges, while the north and south plunge into rugged mountains with sheer vertical drops.

Strong walls and towers girdle the summit, with only narrow gates as entry points.Its altitude and placement gave the Marathas a commanding view of the surrounding valleys, making it both a watchtower and a bulwark.

There is the Kondaneshwar temple, dedicated to Lord Shiva, dating back to the Yadava period (12th–13th century). To the left of Kondaneshwar stands another ancient shrine, dedicated to Lord Shiva in his Amruteshwar form.

The Kalyan Gate is one of the fort’s main approaches, adorned with sculptures of elephants, symbolizing strength and guardianship. There is also a memorial to Udai Bhan Rathore, who feel fighting to defend the fort. From Udai Bhan’s memorial, descending to the fort walls gives a sweeping view of Purandar, Rajgad, and Torna forts.

Another memorial built for Chhatrapati Rajaram in Rajasthani-style architecture, it honors Shivaji Maharaj’s younger son, who passed away here on March 2, 1700.

**History**

The fort was originally under the control of the Mahadev Kolis, ruled by their chieftain Nagnath Nayak. In 1360, Muhammad bin Tughlaq, having shifted his capital to Devagiri (Daulatabad) to guard against Mongol invasions, tried to extend his grip southward. The Kolis held out for a full year against the Sultan’s forces. The geography of the for, its sheer cliffs, ridges, and limited access point,made it a nightmare for invaders.

With supplies dwindling and the siege unsustainable, Tughlaq abandoned the attempt and returned to Delhi. The Kolis reclaimed Kondhana, proving that local chieftains could resist even the might of the Delhi Sultanate.

After centuries under the Mahadev Kolis, the fort was eventually captured by the Nizam Shahis of Ahmednagar. By 1647, they had turned Kondhana into a military base, recognizing its strategic importance in the Sahyadris. In 1665, Shivaji was compelled to sign the Treaty of Purandar with Raja Jai Singh of Amber, one of Aurangzeb’s most trusted generals. As part of the treaty, several forts,including Kondhana,had to be surrendered to the Mughals.

Jijabai’s insistence on reclaiming Kondhana wasn’t just about a fort, it was about pride, identity, and the Maratha spirit itself. For her, losing Kondhana meant losing dignity itself.She pressed Shivaji to gift her the fort, seeing it as a matter of honor.

Shivaji Maharaj however was hesitant, he knew the risks, the fort was impregnable, and Udai Bhan, the presiding commander was a veteran known for his discipline, backed up by a strong garrison of Mughal, Rajput, and Pathan soldiers.

**Tanaji Malusare**

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!7Lcl!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd2d5a119-d81c-431d-9f05-e2a2506f29e0_1024x1024.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!7Lcl!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd2d5a119-d81c-431d-9f05-e2a2506f29e0_1024x1024.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

But he gave in to his mother’s request, and he called his childhood friend, and one of his ablest men, Tanaji Malusare a giant of a man, and a fiercely loyal general, for the mission. He had however come not as a warrior, but as a friend, inviting Shivaji to his son’s wedding.

*“As long as Tanaji is alive, Shivaji will not need to go to Kondana, we shall take the fort” .*

Out of respect for Tanaji’s family occasion, Shivaji intended to postpone the assault on Kondhana until after the wedding. Instead, Tanaji insisted on leading the mission immediately. His loyalty and courage transformed a personal visit into a legendary campaign.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ybtJ!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fbd62d988-c249-4b02-a026-b85272dfc627_537x357.jpeg)

![sin1](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ybtJ!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fbd62d988-c249-4b02-a026-b85272dfc627_537x357.jpeg "sin1")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**The Mission**

The mission however was suicidal in many ways. The fort’s vertical drops made scaling nearly impossible, especially under cover of darkness. The entrances were heavily fortified, watched by vigilant soldiers.

Udai Bhan commanded a mixed force of Mughal, Rajput, and Pathan warriors, disciplined and battle-hardened. And he was no ordinary commander, an able Rajput commander, respected even by Shivaji, which made the challenge even greater.

Tanaji Malusare’s genius was in surveying the terrain and finding the one weak point, the **southern side of the fort**, less guarded due to its treacherous cliffs. What seemed like a natural barrier became the Marathas’ entry point.

His plan was as daring as it was desperate. He chose the one approach everyone thought impossibl, the **southern cliff**, sheer and forbidding, nearly impossible to climb. He gathered about 1,000 Malvis (Maratha soldiers). His plan was to scale the cliff at night through the **southwestern gorge**, the least defended point.

Once Tanaji and his men broke into the fort, they would rush to the gates. At that signal, another contingent of Marathas waiting outside would storm in, overwhelming the defenders.

It’s believed that Tanaji used his pet ghorpad( Monitory lizard) by typing a rope around it’s waist, and made it to climb up the cliff, and get a foothold. However this seems more a folk tale, and not a true account.

The more recorded historical account is Tanaji and the Malvis, scaling the cliff at a near-vertical **90-degree ascent**, in the dead of night. Using ropes and iron spikes, they secured themselves step by step, then lowered ropes for others to follow.

The climb was silent, perilous, and demanded absolute disciplin, one slip could have betrayed the mission. Once inside, they rushed to the gates, opening them for the waiting Maratha contingent outside.

The moment the Marathas broke into Kondhana, the silence of the night shattered. The garrison, Arabs, Afghans, Rajputs, Pathans,roused in confusion, but quickly rallied under Udai Bhan’s command. Outnumbered, the Marathas fought with the desperation of men who knew retreat was impossible.

The clash of steel rang through the fort as torches flared, shadows leaping across the walls. The defenders surged, but the Marathas pressed forward like a storm. At the center of it all was Tanaji Malusare, fighting like a man possessed. His sword cut through ranks, his presence igniting his men to fight beyond exhaustion. Every step he took carved a path of defiance.

As the garrison reeled under the Maratha onslaught, Udai Bhan himself entered the fray. A seasoned Rajput commander, he was determined to hold Kondhana at all costs. The two warriors clashed in a furious duel. Tanaji’s shield shattered under the blows, yet he refused to yiel, wrapping a cloth around his arm and head to serve as a makeshift guard.

Wounded but relentless, Tanaji fought with the ferocity of a lion. Udai Bhan matched him stroke for stroke. Neither man gave ground, neither accepted defeat. Tanaji fell, mortally wounded. Udai Bhan too succumbed to his injuries. Two great warriors perished in the same moment, locked in a duel that symbolized courage beyond life itself.

Just as despair threatened to overwhelm the Marathas after Tanaji’s fall, his brother Suryaji appeared with the reserve force of Mavals. Seeing Tanaji dead, the Mavals faltered, fear gripping them as they began to retreat. He blocked their exit, declaring there was no way ou, *“either victory or death.”* His words transformed panic into fury.

The Mavals, cornered and desperate, roared *“Har Har Mahadev!”* and hurled themselves upon the garrison with unstoppable ferocity.So furious was their assault that many defenders, unable to withstand the charge, leapt over the walls in desperatio, only to meet death on the rocks below.

When Shivaji Maharaj arrived at the fort, he saw not triumph but tragedy—his closest friend, his childhood companion, and one of his ablest generals lying dead. The fort was theirs, but the price was unbearable. Shivaji refused to celebrate, his grief overwhelming him. *“Gad ala, pan Sinha gela” ,“The fort is captured, but the lion is lost”* became immortal, echoing through generations as the essence of Maratha valor and loyalty.

Lokamanya Bal Gangadhar Tilak, one of the foremost leaders of the independence movement, stayed at the bungalow on Sinhagad. Mahatma Gandhi too is said to have drawn from Sinhagad’s spirit. Whenever he was in Pune, he would ask for water from the *Devtak tank* behind Tanaji’s memoria, a quiet act that linked him to the fort’s legacy of sacrifice.

Sinhagad is one of those places where geography and memory fuse into something almost overwhelming. Standing on its walls, the Sahyadri ranges stretch out in majestic grandeu, rugged ridges, plunging cliffs, and valleys that seem endless. The air itself carries echoes of history.

When you look down those sheer drops, it’s impossible not to imagine the Maratha soldiers clambering up in pitch darkness, ropes biting into stone, hearts pounding with the knowledge that one slip meant death. That vision alone sends a chill up your spine.

The grueling hike from Khadakwasla to Sinhagad fort isn’t just physical endurance, it’s a rite of passage. Every cadet who climbs those cliffs retraces, in spirit, the footsteps of Tanaji and his Mavals.

The climb is punishing, designed to push cadets to their limits. But it’s also a reminder that courage often begins where comfort ends.

Sinhagad is not just a fort,it’s the site of one of the most daring military operations in Indian history. To reach it after such a climb is to feel, viscerally, the valor of those

Tanaji Malusare, the lion who gave his life, Shivaji Maharaj, the military genius who inspired a nation; and today’s NDA cadets, who guard India’s border, all are linked by the spirit of Sinhagad.

It’s fitting that the fort, once won through sacrifice, now inspires the guardians of modern India. The cry of *“Har Har Mahadev”* that echoed in 1670 finds its echo in the discipline and pride of every cadet who recalls their climb. Sinhagad thus remains not just a monument of the past, but a living torch of courage carried into the present.
